title: New England clam chowder
subtitle: Creamy clam and potato soup with bacon and thyme
excerpt: The white New England clam chowder: clams cooked in their own liquor with potato, celery, onion, and back bacon, then enriched with cream. Thick enough to be a meal on its own, served with crusty bread or oyster crackers. The standard against which all other chowders are measured.
sourceNotes: New England clam chowder is documented in American cookery literature from the eighteenth century. An early printed recipe appears in the Boston Post (1751) and the dish is described in period New England household recipe collections. Multiple cross-referenced American regional sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cream-based soups do not freeze well — the cream splits on reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the chowder base a day ahead and refrigerate. Add the cream when reheating.

content.text: Fresh clams make the best chowder, but tinned clams in clam juice produce a good result and are significantly more practical. If using fresh clams, steam them in a covered pan with a splash of water for 4 to 5 minutes until they open, then reserve the liquor and remove the clam meat from the shells. The briny, sweet liquor is a large part of the flavour.

New England chowder is cream-based and white. It is thickened by the starch from the potato as it breaks down during cooking, with a small amount of flour cooked into the bacon fat to help. The cream goes in at the end and should not boil once it is added — a boiling cream soup splits and looks curdled.

items.prepNote: fresh in shell, scrubbed, or 400 g tinned clams in brine
peeled and cut into 1.5 cm cubes
or clam juice from tinned clams

content.text: If using fresh {{clams}}: place them in a large saucepan with 100 ml of water, cover tightly, and steam over high heat for 4 to 5 minutes until all the shells have opened. Discard any that remain closed. Strain the liquor through a fine sieve and reserve. Remove the clam meat from the shells. If using tinned clams, drain and reserve the brine.

In a large saucepan over medium heat, cook the {{back-bacon}} of bacon lardons until the fat has rendered and the bacon is lightly golden. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, then the {{onion}} onion and {{celery}} celery sticks and cook for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{plain-flour}} of flour and stir for 2 minutes. Gradually pour in the {{stock-fish}} of fish stock (or reserved clam liquor) and {{whole-milk}} of milk, stirring constantly. Add the {{potato}} of potato cubes, {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves, and {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the potatoes are tender.

Remove the bay leaves. Stir in the {{double-cream}} of double cream and bring just to a simmer — do not boil. Add the clam meat and heat through for 2 minutes. Taste and adjust the seasoning; chowder needs more salt than you expect. Serve with bread or crackers.

items.symptom: Chowder has split and looks curdled
items.cause: Cream brought to a hard boil after adding
items.fix: Once the cream is in, keep the heat low — a gentle simmer only; vigorous boiling splits the cream

items.cause: Potatoes not broken down, or not enough flour in the base
items.fix: Mash a quarter of the potatoes against the side of the pan with a spoon to release starch and thicken the soup

items.cause: Overcooked — clams need minimal heat once added
items.fix: Add the clams in the last 2 minutes only, just enough to warm through; long cooking makes them tough

content.text: New England clam chowder is the white, cream-based version that comes from the northeastern United States — Massachusetts, Rhode Island, Maine, Connecticut. It is distinct from Manhattan clam chowder, which is tomato-based and associated with the New York tradition. In the United States, the debate between the two styles is long-standing and occasionally heated. The New England version is older by most accounts and is the one served in a sourdough bread bowl at the Fisherman's Wharf in San Francisco, which has made it internationally familiar.
